natural_occurrence.description: Phosphoric acid does not occur naturally in free form. Phosphates (salts of phosphoric acid) are abundant in nature, found in rocks, bones, and as essential components of all living cells (DNA, ATP, cell membranes).

manufacturing.description: Commercially produced by two main methods: (1) Wet process - treating phosphate rock (calcium phosphate) with sulfuric acid, or (2) Thermal process - burning elemental phosphorus in air to form phosphorus pentoxide, then hydrating it. Food-grade phosphoric acid uses the thermal process for higher purity. Global production exceeds 40 million tonnes annually, primarily for fertilizers.

other_uses.cosmetics: pH adjuster in skincare and dental products.
other_uses.industrial: Rust removal, metal treatment, fertilizer production (85% of production), detergents, water treatment.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: pH adjuster, dental etchant, pharmaceutical intermediate.

eu.conditions: Quantum satis in many categories; specific limits in others due to phosphate intake concerns

regulatory_history.event: EFSA expressed concern about high dietary phosphate intake in Western diets, particularly from food additives. Recommended monitoring and potential reduction of phosphate use in processed foods.

commonly_found_in: Cola beverages (Coca-Cola, Pepsi - gives characteristic tangy taste)
fun_facts: Phosphoric acid is the secret ingredient that gives Coca-Cola and other colas their distinctive tangy, slightly sharp taste
Strong enough to dissolve rust, which is why it's used in metal cleaning products
Despite concerns about dietary phosphate overload in Western diets, phosphoric acid itself is safe when used in regulated amounts
Your body needs phosphorus (from phosphates) for bone health, DNA, and energy production (ATP)
One can of cola contains about 40-70 mg of phosphoric acid
